segments.s216-g129: دو منزل = a noun phrase with the numeral before the noun: منزل here means 'stage (of a journey)'. همی‌کرد is the classical imperfective/past-continuous auxiliary meaning 'was doing' or 'kept on doing' (an archaic poetic form), and it appears before the subject in this inverted word order. رستم is the subject; یکی (literally 'one') refers back to 'two stages' and functions as 'one (of them)'—it prepares the contrast completed by the next segment.
segments.s216-g130: نیاسود is the negative past of آسودن ('to rest/ be at ease') and means 'did not rest'; the implied subject is Rostam from the previous clause. روز و شبان is a time-expression 'day and night' (an idiomatic pairing; شبان is an archaic/poetic form here), placed after the verb as the temporal complement. اندکی is an adverb 'for a little/a short while' modifying the duration; together the segment says that, on that one stage, he did not rest day or night for a short time.
segments.s216-g113: فریبرز را گفت برکش پگاه: فریبرز را is the indirect‑object / topicalized phrase 'to Fariburz' (را marks the definite/accusative role). گفت is the main verb 'said' (perfect/past). برکش پگاه is an imperative/infinitival command phrase: پگاه 'dawn' (time adverbial) follows the verb, so the clause reads 'said to Fariburz: rise/advance at dawn.'
segments.s216-g114: سپاه اندر آور به پیش سپاه: سپاه اندر آور is a compound verbal phrase meaning 'bring/raise the army' (سپاه = 'army', اندر آور = 'bring in/bring forward'). به پیش سپاه is a locative/goal phrase 'to the front of the host' (به + پیش = 'towards the front/of the presence of'). Word order is verb phrase then directional phrase.
segments.s216-g115: نباید که روز و شبان بغنوی: نباید is an impersonal negative 'one must not / you must not'. که introduces the subordinate clause. روز و شبان is a paired time expression 'day and night' (poetic formation). بغنوی is a subjunctive/imperative negative 'do not sleep' (from غنودن 'to fall asleep'), so the whole clause means 'you must not sleep day and night.'
segments.s216-g116: مگر نزد طوس سپهبد شوی: مگر here = 'unless'. نزد + name = 'in the presence/at the side of'. طوس is the proper name (Tous, the hero). سپهبد شوی is a copular/result phrase 'become spahbed' (شوی is subjunctive/ present of شدن 'to become'). Together: 'unless you become spahbed at Tous's side.'
segments.s216-g117: بگویی که در جنگ تندی مکن: بگویی is a hortative/imperative 'say/tell (him)' (introducing reported counsel). که introduces the content. در جنگ 'in battle' is locative, and تندی مکن is a negated command 'do not be hasty' (تندی = haste/fierceness; مکن = 'do not (do)'). Word order places the setting before the prohibition.
segments.s216-g118: فریب زمان جوی و کندی مکن: this contains two coordinated admonitions. فریب زمان جوی is best read as 'do not seek (be taken by) the deception/enticement of time' — جوی (here) functions as 'seek/search' and فریب is the noun 'deception/enticement'. And کندی مکن is another negated command 'do not be slow' (کندی = slowness; مکن = do not). The conjunction و links the two prohibitions.
segments.s216-g119: من اینک به کردار باد دمان: من اینک = 'I now'. به کردار = 'in the manner/character of' (به + noun phrase). باد here functions optatively/figuratively ('like the wind' or 'as the wind'); دمان (دامن) = 'skirt/edge' (poetic geographic image). The whole phrase is metaphorical: 'I now, in the manner of the wind, (to) the skirt/edge...'—setting up the speaker's intended swift action.
segments.s216-g120: بیایم نجویم بره بر زمان: بیایم is a volitional/subjunctive 'may I come / I shall come' (first‑person subjunctive/optative). نجویم is negated here 'I will not seek' (from جستن/جویدن); بره 'lamb' is figurative 'a tame/innocent creature', and بر زمان 'before/against time' = 'in relation to fate/time'. Together: 'I will come and will not seek to be a lamb before time' (i.e. I will not act like a submissive/helpless creature facing fate).
segments.s216-g57: ز من = a poetic prepositional phrase 'from me' (z + pronoun). هرچ باید فزونی بخواه is a nominal + modal + imperative construction: هرچ 'whatever (is) necessary' + باید 'it is necessary/ought' qualifying that 'فزونی' (an increase/addition) is needed, and بخواه is an imperative 'ask/seek' commanding the addressee to request it from the speaker. Word order places the qualifier باید before the noun فزونی and the imperative at the end.
segments.s216-g58: A simple list introduced by ز 'from' (poetic) that names the categories to be requested: اسپ 'horse/steed', سلیح 'weapon(s)', and after another ز, گنج 'treasure' and سپاه 'army/troops'. The repeated preposition z marks each source/item class.
segments.s216-g59: برو is the imperative 'go'. با introduces a manner phrase 'with' and governs two coordinated noun–adjective phrases: دلی شاد 'a joyful/heartful heart' (دلی = 'a heart' used adverbially) and رایی درست 'a correct/sound counsel'. The coordination (و) links the two manners.
segments.s216-g60: نشاید is an impersonal negative/prescriptive particle meaning 'it is not fitting/should not'. گرفت here is best read as passive or resultative 'be taken/undertaken' (i.e., 'be done'). The clause order is verb (نشاید گرفت) then the demonstrative modifier این‌چنین + noun کار 'deed/affair' with سست 'weak/lax' as its adjective: 'it is not fitting that such a weak deed be undertaken.'
segments.s216-g61: به پاسخ 'in reply' is an adverbial phrase; چنین 'thus' modifies the reported speech. گفت is the past-tense verb 'said' (here indexed by the available sense entry), with the speaker رستم as subject and به شاه marking the indirect object 'to the king'. The word order places the report (به پاسخ چنین) before گفت.
segments.s216-g62: که introduces the content of Rostam's utterance. بی تو 'without you' + مبادا is an optative/negative jussive meaning 'may it not be / let it not happen', followed by the nouns نگین و کلاه ('a gem and a crown') as the things he wishes not to exist without the king. The structure is a negative wish rather than a plain negation.
segments.s216-g63: This is a coordinated list of qualities introduced by با 'with': فر 'farr/royal glory', برزی (an older term; no matching sense in the provided list so marked -1) which here functions as another exalted quality, and با‌رای 'with counsel/opinion' and داد 'justice'. The coordination links attributes that characterize the kind of king being spoken of.
segments.s216-g64: ندارد is the present-tense 'does not have/possess'; چو is a comparative 'as/like', so چو تو شاه = 'a king such as you'. گردون 'the heavens/sky (often = fate/world)' is the subject/contextual locus, and به‌یاد here means 'remembering' or 'that are remembered'. Together they form 'the heavens do not (recall/possess) a king like you' — i.e., there is no remembered king like you endowed with those qualities.
segments.s216-g105: This short phrase names the addressee/vocative first (فریبرز) and then the recipient marked by the object marker را (کاوس را), followed by the quantity + noun (ده سپاه). In conversational/poetic Persian the imperative verb 'give' is omitted and understood; the sequence implies a command: 'Fereyberz, give Kay Kāvus ten armies.'
segments.s216-g106: که introduces a subordinate clause of purpose/result here ('so that'). او is the third-person pronoun ('he') referring back to the vocative Fereyberz. پیش‌رو is an adjectival role phrase 'vanguard/foremost'. باشد is the present subjunctive/optative sense 'may be' (used after که to express purpose/wish). و connects the two predicates, and کینه‌خواه is a nominal adjective 'avenger' describing the same person.
segments.s216-g107: Subject (proper name) تهمتن comes first, then the direct object زمین marked by را ('the ground'). ببوسید is simple past third-person 'he kissed'. و links the two verbs; گفت is the past 'he said' introducing reported speech that follows.
segments.s216-g108: که here introduces reported content ('that'). با من 'with me' sets the co-participant. عنان ('reins') and the coordinated رکیب‌ست جفت form the predication: رکیب‌ست is a fused form meaning 'is (a) rider/pertaining to a rider' and جفت is 'paired/matched'. The clause states that reins and rider are paired in relation to the speaker.
segments.s216-g109: This phrase uses the structure 'N1 را N2' where سران را (the leaders) is followed by سر (head) to mean 'the heads of the leaders' — a common Persian appositional/partitive pattern. اندر شتاب 'in haste' (اندر = in/into, شتاب = haste) gives the manner, and آوریم is first-person plural subjunctive/optative 'let us bring' or 'we shall bring', used here as an exhortation.
segments.s216-g110: مبادا introduces a negative purpose/lest-clause ('lest, may it not be that'). که introduces the content. آرام و خواب are coordinated nouns 'peace/calm and sleep'; آوریم repeats the subjunctive/optative 'we bring' meaning 'we might bring' or 'we would cause'. Together the clause warns against causing peace and sleep (i.e. letting them rest).
segments.s216-g111: سپه را marks the beneficiary/affected party ('to the army') with را. درم دادن is an infinitival/verbal-noun phrase 'to give/distribute dirhams (pay)'; آغاز کرد is the past 'he began'. The sequence ('درم دادن آغاز کرد') is the classic Persian periphrastic construction 'began to do X'.
segments.s216-g112: به دشت آمد is a simple locative motion clause: به + place (دشت, 'plain') + آمد 'he came'. و links the clauses. رزم را ساز کرد uses ساز ('device/arrangement' here) with کردن to mean 'made/arranged the battle' — idiomatically 'prepared for battle'; را again marks رزم as the object of ساز کردن.
segments.s216-g81: 'ازان کشتگان' is a causal/partitive phrase 'from/for those slain'; it sets the cause or reference. 'شاه' is the patient/subject (the king). 'بی‌درد' is an adjectival predicate meaning 'without pain / without sorrow'. Final 'باد' is the optative particle ('may it be'), so the whole clause = 'May the king be without sorrow because of those slain.' Word order is slightly inverted (prepositional phrase + subject + predicate) as is common in poetry.
segments.s216-g82: Literal order 'رخ بدسگالان او زرد باد' = 'May the face(s) of his bad‑omened ones be pale.' 'رخ' (face) is the theme, followed by its modifier 'بدسگالان او' (the ill‑omened ones of his / his ill‑omened men) where possession is expressed by juxtaposition. 'زرد' is the adjective 'pale/wan' and again 'باد' is the optative particle giving the wish. The phrase is a parallel optative clause to the first.
segments.s216-g83: 'ز' here marks cause or motive ('because of / on account of'). 'گودرزیان خود' means 'my own Gudarzis' with 'خود' intensifying the possessive. 'جگر خسته‌ام' is an idiomatic copular clause: literally 'my liver is tired' = 'my heart/heartfelt feeling is weary'; '-ام' is the first‑person present copula ('I am').
segments.s216-g84: 'کمر بستن' is an idiom 'to gird one's loins / to brace oneself'; here 'کمر' (waist) is the noun, and the verb 'بسته‌ام' (perfect / resultative: 'I have bound') takes it. 'بر میان سوگ' is a locative phrase 'in the middle of mourning' ('بر' = on/upon, 'میان' = middle, 'سوگ' = mourning). The enclitic 'را' marks the definite/affected noun ('سوگ را') and the verb is first person 'I have bound', so overall 'I have girded my waist in the midst of mourning.'
segments.s216-g85: 'چو' is the temporal conjunction 'when'. 'بشنید' is the past simple 'he heard'. 'کیخسرو' is the subject (proper name). 'آواز اوی' = 'his voice' (possession indicated by the enclitic form). Note the verb-before-subject order ('چو بشنید کیخسرو') — a poetic inversion that is common in classical Persian.
segments.s216-g86: 'به رخ برنهاد' literally 'he lifted toward (his) face' — idiomatically 'he lifted his countenance / raised his face'. 'برنهاد' is past 'raised up/placed up'. 'از دو دیده دو جوی' is a compact verbless clause after that: 'from two eyes (came) two streams' — the first 'دو' counts the eyes, the second 'دو' counts the streams; classical Persian often omits the copula/verb in such pictorial lines.
segments.s216-g87: 'بدو' = 'to him' (indirect object). 'گفت' is the past tense 'he said' (introducing reported speech). 'بی‌تو' = 'without you'. 'نخواهم' is negative present/future 'I will not want / I want not'. 'زمان' here is used idiomatically ('time' / 'the world / life') — together 'بی‌تو نخواهم زمان' = 'without you I care for nothing / I want no life/time.'
segments.s216-g88: This is a correlative negation with 'نه ... و نه ...' = 'neither ... nor ...'. The nouns list worldly symbols: 'اورنگ' (throne/worldly glory), 'تاج' (crown), 'گرز' (mace) and 'کمان' (bow). There is no verb here because the negated desire from the previous segment carries over: 'I want neither throne nor crown, nor mace nor bow.'
segments.s216-g73: The paired particle هم ... هم introduces a correlative 'both ... and' construction. ز/از is the poetic preposition meaning 'from' or 'because of' and precedes the nouns تشنگی and راه دراز; دراز is a postpositive adjective modifying راه (long road). Word order is 'both from X and from Y'.
segments.s216-g74: گزیدن here is used metaphorically like English 'to taste' or 'to undergo' (i.e., to experience suffering). در رنج is a prepositional phrase 'in suffering/hardship'; بر جای ناز means 'in place of nâz' (i.e. instead of pampering/comfort). The infinitive/masdar-like verb گزیدن functions as a verbal noun describing the experience.
segments.s216-g75: چنین is a demonstrative meaning 'such' or 'so'; it modifies the coordinated nouns درد و سختی. بسی is an adverbial intensifier meaning 'much' or 'very many'. The verb دیده‌ام (perfect 1st person) comes at the end, following the object phrase in typical Persian SOV order.
segments.s216-g76: که introduces a result/consequence clause 'that/so that'. روزی here functions adverbially ('ever/once/at any time' in classical usage) and precedes the negated perfect نپرسیده‌ام ('I have not asked/inquired'); ز (poetic from/of) marks the object شادی ('joy'). The negative prefix ن- attaches to پرسیده‌ام to make 'I have not asked'.
segments.s216-g77: This is a paired clause: 'تو شاه نو آیین' is a nominative phrase ('you, king of new custom' = 'you newly enthroned king'); و links clauses. من چون رهی is a simile 'I like/as a wayfarer' with چون meaning 'like'. Word order places the descriptive noun phrases before the verb that follows in the next segment.
segments.s216-g78: میان بسته‌ام is a perfect form (بسته‌ام) with میان as its object/role meaning 'I have shut/bound the middle' or idiomatically 'I am hemmed in between.' چون تو فرمان دهی is a temporal/subordinate clause 'when/whenever you give the command' where دهی is the present-subjunctive/optative 2nd/1st person form 'you give' used in dependent temporal clauses.
segments.s216-g79: شوم is an optative/subjunctive first-person form meaning 'may I become' or 'let me'; it introduces a wish or resolution. با سپاهی is an instrumental phrase 'with an army'. کمر بر میان is an idiomatic cluster: کمر (waist/loins) with بر میان ('to the middle/into the fray') implies girding oneself for battle; the finishing verb (to act) appears in the next segment.
segments.s216-g80: بگردانم is a first-person subjunctive/optative of گرداندن 'to turn/repel' and completes the wish begun with شوم ('may I turn...'). این بد is the object 'this misfortune/evil', and ز ایرانیان uses poetic ز for 'from/away from the Iranians' (i.e., to remove the evil from the Iranians). Word order is verb–object–prepositional phrase.
segments.s216-g41: هرانکس is an indefinite relative ‘whoever’. کزیشان is a contracted poetic form (که + از + ایشان) meaning ‘who from them’/‘among them’. به جان رسته‌اند is a perfect/resultive verb phrase: رسته‌اند (perfect, 3rd plural) ‘have escaped / been delivered’, with the phrase به جان functioning idiomatically (‘by/with life’) — به here marks the means/instrument (archaic/poetic use).
segments.s216-g42: به marks direction/place here (‘to/toward’), introducing the locative phrase کوه هماون (a named mountain; هماون functions like a demonstrative/name). همه is the subject ‘all/everyone’, and خسته‌اند is the predicate (3rd plural present/perfect-state) ‘are tired/worn out’ placed at the end as is normal in Persian poetic word order.
segments.s216-g43: همه is the subject ‘all/everyone’. سر نهاده is a perfect/perfect-participle construction literally ‘having placed (their) head(s)’ — an idiom meaning ‘bowed their heads’; Persian often omits the possessive pronoun here. سوی آسمان is a prepositional phrase ‘toward heaven/sky’ following the predicate.
segments.s216-g44: سوی begins another directional phrase ‘toward’. کردگار is the divine addressee ‘the Creator/God’, followed by مکان و زمان — two nouns linked by و in apposition functioning as epithets describing the Creator (‘of place and of time’). There is no new verb; this phrase continues the directional notion of the previous segment.
segments.s216-g45: که is the subordinating particle ‘that’/introducing a clause. ایدر is an adverb ‘here’. بیاید is a subjunctive/jussive form ‘may come / should come’ (present subjunctive used to express wish/command). گو is a poetic/formative element meaning ‘so‑called / called’ (used with the following name), and پیلتن is the place/name; together گو پیلتن = ‘called Piltan’ (a naming apposition).
segments.s216-g46: به marks the instrument/means here (‘by’/‘through’). نیروی یزدان is a genitive phrase ‘the power/force of God’ and فرمان من is ‘my command’ (فرمان followed by the postposed pronoun من = ‘my’). The two instruments are linked by و, giving ‘by the power of God and by my command’.
segments.s216-g47: شب تیره is a noun phrase ‘dark night’. کاین is a contracted poetic form (که + این) introducing the subordinate temporal clause ‘when/that this …’. نامه‌بر is the object ‘the letter‑carrier / messenger’. خواندم is past tense 1st singular; here it functions as ‘I summoned/called’ (خواندن can mean ‘to call’ in older/poetic usage), so the clause means ‘when I summoned this messenger’.
segments.s216-g48: بسی is an older adverbial/intensifier here meaning ‘much’/‘very much’. از is the preposition ‘from/out of’. جگر is figurative ‘heart/seat of feeling’ (not the anatomical liver in literal sense). خون is ‘blood’ and برافشاندم is a past transitive verb ‘I scattered/poured out’ — together a poetic expression ‘I poured out much blood from my heart’ (hyperbolic for intense grief).
segments.s216-g1: ازان پس (literally 'from that then') is a temporal connective meaning 'after that' or 'thereupon'. چو آمد is a temporal clause marker + past verb meaning 'when (it) came'; the subject of آمد is implicit 'news' (خبر). به‌خسرو places the recipient with the preposition به 'to' (to Khosrow/the king). خبر is the noun 'news' that arrived.
segments.s216-g2: که introduces a subordinate content clause 'that ...'. پیران is the plural noun 'the elders/old men' (here: the group who fought). شد is the simple past 'became' (not auxiliary). از رزم literally 'from the battle' functions adverbially 'in/through the fighting'. پیروزگر is an adjective/epithet meaning 'victorious' (here describing their state).
segments.s216-g3: سپهبد is the subject, the military rank 'spahbed' (a general). به‌کوه هماون is a prepositional phrase 'to the mountain Homāvan' (به = to, کوه = mountain; هماون is the mountain name or demonstrative identifying which mountain). کشید is the past of کشیدن used here intransitively 'moved/drew/withdrew' (poetic usage).
segments.s216-g4: ز لشکر 'from the army/host' marks the group affected. بسی is an intensive/quantifying word 'many/a great number' (poetic); گرد here denotes units/troops or the notion of grouping, and شد ناپدید together mean 'became invisible/vanished' — i.e. many of the host were scattered and disappeared. The clause is descriptive of the army's dispersal.
segments.s216-g5: در کاخ گودرز is a locative phrase 'in the palace of Goudarz' (در = in; کاخ is 'palace'; گودرز is the proper name in genitive position). کشوادگان is an archaic/plural noun referring to the household, retainers or followers associated with the house (an old/formal term placed after the location).
segments.s216-g6: تهی شد is a passive/stative expression 'became empty/was emptied'. ز introduces the source 'of/from' (here 'of troops and free men'). گردان means 'troops/units' and آزادگان 'the free/men of status' — together indicating what the palace was emptied of.
segments.s216-g7: ستاره is the subject 'the star' (celestial). بر ایشان is a prepositional phrase 'over them' (بر = over/on; ایشان = them). بنالد is the present/habitual poetic form of نالیدن 'to moan/complain/weep' (here narrative: 'lamented'), and همی is the classical narrative particle marking continuous or emphatic action ('then/indeed/continually').
segments.s216-g8: به‌بالینشان is a locative phrase 'at their bedside' (بالین = bedside; the prefix به marks direction/location). خون is the noun 'blood' (here meaning blood that appears/flows). بپالد is a poetic verb form (ب‑ + پالَدن) — in this context it describes blood rising/welling or being produced at the bedside (a vivid, somewhat archaic poetic verb); همی again gives an ongoing/emphatic sense. (The exact nuance of پالیدن is archaic/poetic and here best rendered as 'well up' or 'spring forth'.)
segments.s216-g49: نگفتم is the past negative first‑person of گفتن: “I did not say/tell.” The time phrase سه روز follows the verb and means “for three days.” این سخن را is the definite object (را marks the object) “these/this words,” and به کس means “to anyone/to anyone at all” — the whole segment: “I did not tell these words to anyone for three days.”
segments.s216-g50: مگر here means “except.” پیش takes the sense “in the presence of / before,” so پیش دادار فریادرس = “before the Creator who answers cries.” فریادرس is an epithet of دادار (appositive), and no new finite verb appears — the clause is an exception to the previous negative statement.
segments.s216-g51: کنون is 'now.' کار is the subject 'the affair/work.' ز اندازه اندر گذشت is an idiomatic past construction: ز + اندازه = 'beyond measure,' اندر گذشت = past 'passed/has gone' — together 'the affair has passed beyond measure' (i.e. exceeded bounds). گذشت is third‑person past.
segments.s216-g52: دلم is دل + possessive suffix -م = 'my heart.' زین سخن = 'because of/from this speech/these words' (ز in poetic register = 'from/because of'). پر ز تیمار گشت is 'became full of care/worry': پر = 'full (of)', ز + تیمار = 'of care/worry', گشت = past 'became.' The word order places the possessor/subject first, then the causal prepositional phrase, then predicate.
segments.s216-g53: امید سپاه و سپهبد is a nominal phrase 'the hope of the army and the spahbed.' به تست expresses that this hope 'is upon you' (به = 'in/on/to'; تست is an archaic / contracted form used here in the sense 'is in/rests on you' — a relic of old-fashioned copula/possessive construction). The clause therefore means 'the army's and the spahbed's hope is in you.'
segments.s216-g54: که introduces the following wish/explanatory clause 'that / namely.' روشن‌روان is a compound adjective 'bright‑souled, cheerful.' بادی here is an optative/archaic verbal form meaning 'may you be' (the same optative force as باد in blessings), so روشن‌روان بادی = 'may you be bright‑souled.' و تن درست adds 'and (may) your body be sound/healthy' (درست = 'sound/proper').
segments.s216-g55: سرت = سر + second‑person suffix (-ت) = 'your head' (in Persian blessing formulas this idiomatically means 'may you live long'). سبز is the adjective 'green.' باد is the optative particle 'may (it be)' so سرت سبز باد = 'may your head be green.' و دلت شادمان = 'and may your heart (دلت, دل + -ت) be joyful' — shadman is an adjective 'joyful.'
segments.s216-g56: This is another blessing clause with an implied optative: تن زال = 'the body of Zāl' (noun + name in genitive apposition), دور از = 'far from,' بد = 'evil/ill,' and بدگمان = 'ill‑thought / suspicion / one of ill opinion.' Together: 'may Zāl's body be far from evil and from suspicion.' The finite verb is omitted but understood from the previous optative context.
segments.s216-g65: شنیده‌ست is a perfect/perfective construction: شنیده (past participle) + poetic suffix -ست (contraction of است), yielding “has heard.” The subject خسرو follows the verb (verb–subject order, common in epic/classical Persian for emphasis). که introduces the reported subordinate clause. تا here functions as an emphatic/limiting particle meaning roughly “even/as far as,” linking the report to the named king کیقباد.
segments.s216-g66: This is the reported clause: کلاه بزرگی ('a large hat') + بسر (an older fused form of به سر, 'on the head') + بر نهاد (compound verb بر نهادن in past tense, 'placed on/put on'). Word order is noun phrase → locative (بسر) → verb; the possessive (his) is omitted and understood from context. Note: بسر is an archaic condensed form of به سر.
segments.s216-g67: به‌ایران and به‌کین are fronted adverbial phrases: به‌ایران 'for/to Iran' (purpose/concern) and به‌کین 'in vengeance/for revenge' (manner/reason). من is the explicit pronoun for emphasis. کمر بسته‌ام is a perfec t/resultative form: کمر بستن (literally 'to bind the waist') is an idiom meaning 'to gird oneself' or 'to prepare for action'; بسته‌ام is past participle + suffix -ام ('I have/ I did').
segments.s216-g68: به آرام ('in peace/quiet') modifies the following clause; یک روز ('one day') is adverbial here meaning 'even a single day.' ننشسته‌ام is the negated perfect: ن- (negative) + نشسته (past participle of نشستن) + ام, yielding 'I have not sat/ I have not rested.' The whole phrase means 'I have not sat a single day in peace.'
segments.s216-g69: A paratactic list of dangers joined by و (and). Each noun stands alone without a governing verb — a common poetic device to heap up images: بیابان (wilderness), تاریکی (darkness), دیو (demon), شیر (lion). The repeated و simply coordinates the items.
segments.s216-g70: The correlative construction چه ... چه ... means 'whether... or...' (both چه mark alternatives). جادو is 'magic.' The second چه is followed by از + noun: چه از here gives an alternative 'or from/of the...' and اژدهای دلیر names the other peril: اژدهای (dragon) with postpositive adjective دلیر ('brave').
segments.s216-g71: همان is a demonstrative/adjectival particle meaning 'that same' or 'the very.' It modifies رزم ('battle/struggle'). توران و مازندران are the two lands linked to specify whose battle it is; in poetic diction the ezāfe is often omitted, so the place names follow the noun directly.
segments.s216-g72: Two noun phrases in apposition: شب تیره ('dark/black night' — noun + adjective) joined by و to گرزهای گران ('the heavy maces'). گرزهای is the plural (گرز + های) and گران is the adjective 'heavy/weighty' following the noun, a common adjective placement in Persian poetry.
segments.s216-g33: تو (you) begins the clause as the subject/vocative; تا here means 'so long as / until' introducing a temporal/conditional clause. برنهادی is the past (perfective) 2nd-person singular of برنهاد 'to place/put on' (here transitive), and the object phrase کلاه به‌مردی follows in the order 'object + goal/recipient': کلاه = 'hat' (direct object) and به‌مردی functions as the recipient/locative 'onto a man' (here not the adverbial 'manfully' but a literal 'to a man').
segments.s216-g34: This is the main clause: نکرد is the negative perfect/past 'did not do' (here used with an action noun 'نگاه' to mean 'did not cast/look'); ایچ is an emphatic negative adverb 'not at all / nothing' placed after the verb for emphasis; دشمن is the subject 'the enemy'; به + ایران is the directional prepositional phrase 'toward Iran'; نگاه is the verbal noun 'a look/glance' that completes the predicate ('did not make a look' → 'did not look').
segments.s216-g35: کنون is a temporal adverb 'now' placed sentence-initially. The following coordinated list گیو و گودرز و طوس و سران strings together two proper names and a final common noun سران 'the chiefs/leaders' — طوس here refers to the hero Tous (proper name) rather than the city.
segments.s216-g36: فراوان functions as a quantifier 'many' / 'numerous' modifying the following noun phrase. ازین is the poetic/contracted form of از + این 'from this' (ین here the orthographic/poetic form of 'این'). مرز is 'frontier/border.' کنداوران is a plural agentive (کند + آور + ان) meaning 'those who dig / excavators' — the whole phrase describes many chiefs/men coming 'from this frontier' who are (literally) diggers or trench-makers; the copula 'are' is omitted (typical in Persian).
segments.s216-g37: همه 'all' quantifies the (implied) people; دل پر از خون is a nominal phrase where پر 'full' takes the measure phrase از خون 'of blood' (i.e. 'heart full of blood' = wounded/grief-filled heart). و links the coordinated descriptive phrase دیده پرآب, where پرآب is a bound compound/adjective 'full of water' idiomatically 'full of tears.' These are attributive phrases describing the people.
segments.s216-g38: گریزان is a present participial/adjectival form meaning 'fleeing' or 'on the run.' ز is the poetic variant of از 'from.' ترکان is the plural noun 'the Turks' and افراسیاب is the genitive/attributive proper name 'of Afrasiab' (i.e. 'the Turks belonging to Afrasiab'). Together they form 'fleeing from Afrasiab's Turks.'
segments.s216-g39: فراوان 'many' quantifies the following group. ز (poetic از) indicates provenance/partitive 'of/from' — 'many of the Goudarzis.' گودرزیان is a nisba plural 'the men/followers of Goudarz.' کشته is a past participle 'killed/slain' that modifies مرد 'men' (کشته مرد = 'men slain' or 'slain men').
segments.s216-g40: شده is the perfect/passive auxiliary 'have become' (here linking the previous clause to its result). خاک بستر is a compound noun 'earth-bed' (خاک = earth, بستر = bed), i.e. 'their bodies became the bed of the earth.' به دشت نبرد is a locative phrase 'on/in the plain of battle' where به marks the spatial relation and نبرد 'battle' qualifies دشت 'plain.' The overall sense: the slain have become the earth's bedding on the battlefield plain.
segments.s216-g9: ازیشان is a fused prepositional/casual phrase (= از + ایشان) meaning 'because of them' placed before the clause to give the cause. جهان پر ز خاک است و خون is a predicative phrase: پر (an adjective 'full (of)') takes the poetic preposition ز + noun (خاک) to mean 'full of dust', followed by the copula است 'is' and و خون 'and blood' accumulated as a second coordinated item. Note the inverted/poetic order (preposition + noun after پر) rather than the modern azar-e ezāfe construction.
segments.s216-g10: بلند اختر طوس is a noun phrase with the adjective بلند 'lofty' modifying اختر 'star', followed by طوس in apposition/genitive (classical Persian often omits an explicit ezāfe). گشته is the perfect/participial verb 'has become/turned', and نگون is a predicate adjective 'downfallen/overthrown' — together 'has become downcast/overthrown'. The sequence is idiomatic poetic perfect: 'گشته + adjective'.
segments.s216-g11: بفرمود is an honorific/past verb 'he said/commanded'. تا functions here as a subordinating conjunction 'that/so that' introducing the subordinate clause whose subject is رستم and whose verb appears in the next segment. پیلتن is a place-name (the destination). The clause literal order is 'he commanded that Rostam (to) Piltan ...'.
segments.s216-g12: خرامد is a past tense verb 'he strode/advanced proudly' agreeing with رستم as its subject; به‌درگاه is the directional prepositional phrase 'to the court/presence', and با انجمن is an adverbial/agent phrase 'with the assembly'. Together they complete the subordinate clause introduced by تا: 'Rostam strode to the court with the assembly.'
segments.s216-g13: برفتند is the past plural verb 'they departed/went'. ز ایران is a prepositional phrase 'from Iran'. همه بخردان is the plural subject/complement 'all the wise (men)'; note the poetic order: verb before its subject ('برفتند ... همه بخردان') is common in epic style. بخردان uses the adjective/epithet sense 'wise/prudent'.
segments.s216-g14: جهاندیده و نامور موبدان is an appositive noun phrase describing those 'wise men': جهاندیده 'worldly‑wise' and نامور 'renowned' both modify موبدان 'mobeds' (Zoroastrian priests). Adjectives precede the noun here as epithets; the whole phrase specifies who the 'wise men' were.
segments.s216-g15: سر نامداران is 'the chief/leader of the famed' (سر = chief, here a title/role). زبان برگشاد is a fixed expression 'opened his tongue' meaning 'began to speak' (زبان = speech/utterance; برگشاد = opened). Grammatically this is subject (سر نامداران) + verb phrase (زبان برگشاد).
segments.s216-g16: ز marks source/subject matter 'about/from' (here 'of the battle'), so ز پیکار لشکر = 'about the army's battle' or 'from the battles of the army' (پیکار لشکر = 'the army's battle'). بسی is an intensifier 'much/many', کرد is the past verb 'made/did', and یاد is the object 'remembrance' — together بسی کرد یاد literally 'made much remembrance', i.e. 'recalled many battles.'
segments.s216-g17: به رستم is a prepositional phrase 'to Rostam' (به = to + proper name). چنین گفت literally 'said thus' — گفت is the past 3rd‑singular of گفتن placed before the vocative. کای is the archaic vocative 'O' and سرفراز is a laudatory adjective/noun 'exalted/victorious' addressing Rostam.
segments.s216-g18: بترسم is an archaic/poetic first‑person present tense expressing 'I fear' (the prefixed بـ occurs in older Persian for the present/subjunctive). که introduces a subordinate clause 'that …'. این دولت = 'this fortune/state'. دیریاز is an old/poetic adjective; its precise nuance is archaic here, so the sentence means 'I fear that this fortune will be short‑lived/come to an end soon.'
segments.s216-g19: همی is an adverb of continued/ongoing action 'still/continually'. برگراید is an archaic present/evitative form meaning 'turns/leans (back) or inclines' (3rd‑singular). به‌سوی نشیب = 'toward decline' (به‌سوی = toward, نشیب = descent/low point).
segments.s216-g20: دلم is 'my heart' (دل + possessive clitic). شد is the past/become auxiliary 'became'. ز کردار او = 'because of his conduct/deeds' (ز = from/because of; کردار = deed; او = he). پرنهیب is a poetic adjective meaning 'filled with dread/alarm' — it describes the resulting state of the heart.
segments.s216-g21: توی here is an archaic/poetic form of address ('thou' / 'in thee') used to call someone; پروارننده is a participial noun 'nurturer, fosterer'. تاج و تخت = 'crown and throne' (two royal symbols in apposition). The whole phrase addresses the addressee as the one who nourishes kingship.
segments.s216-g22: فروغ = 'radiance/splendor'. از تو گیرد is 'takes/draws from you' (از = from; تو = you; گیرد = takes, 3rd‑singular present). جهاندار بخت is a compound epithet 'world‑ruling fortune' or 'the fortune of the world‑ruler' (جهاندار = sovereign/conqueror, بخت = fate/fortune); it is the thing whose radiance is said to be drawn from the addressee.
segments.s216-g23: دل چرخ is a genitive 'the heart of the wheel' where چرخ (here) functions metaphorically as the celestial/fateful wheel. در نوک شمشیر = 'on the point of the sword' (در = on/in, نوک = tip, شمشیر = sword). تست is the archaic copula 'thou art / it is yours' — an old second‑person ending linking the predicate to the addressee.
segments.s216-g24: سپهر و زمان و زمین lists three domains 'heaven/sky, time, and earth' linked by و. زیر تست means 'are under you / beneath you' (زیر here = under/subject to; تست again is the archaic copula relating this subordination to the addressee).
segments.s216-g89: This is an optative clause ending in باد (an optative particle meaning “may it be”). فلک is the subject (“the sky/heaven”) and the prepositional/locative phrase زیر خم کمند تو (literally “under the bend/curve of your lasso”) follows it; تو is the post‑nominal personal pronoun marking possession of کمند (classical/poetic word order).
segments.s216-g90: Another optative clause with باد as wish‑marker: سر تاجداران is a noun phrase ('the heads of the crowned/sovereign ones') with the possessor after the head (poetic omission of ezāfe is common). به بند تو = 'into/into the bond of you' (به + بند) expresses being put in fetters; the whole means 'may the heads of crowned rulers be in your fetters.'
segments.s216-g91: A distributive/poetic list introduced by ز (here 'from'); the preposition ز is repeated for parallelism. The nouns are coordinated by و ('and') and enumerate types of wealth and regalia (دینار, گنج, تاج, گهر). There is no finite verb here; it is a nominal list that links to the surrounding clauses.
segments.s216-g92: Continuation of the inventory begun in the previous segment: a simple coordinated noun sequence (کلاه و کمان و کمند و کمر) linked with و. These are material/equipment items (here 'helmet/hat', 'bow', 'lasso', 'girdle/belt'). As before, no verb is present in the segment itself.
segments.s216-g93: بیاورد here is a past narrative verb ('brought/produced') in third person; word order is verb + nominal sequence. گنجور خسرو is a possessive/attributive juxtaposition ('the treasury of Khosrow' or 'Khosrow's treasury') modifying which key is meant; کلید is the direct object ('the key').
segments.s216-g94: سر بدره‌های درم is the object phrase: بدره‌های درم = 'the (full‑moon–like) dirhams/coins' (a poetic metaphor comparing shiny coins to moons) and سر here means 'heads/tops' of those coins; بردرید is a past tense verb 'cut off / tore off.' The clause describes cutting or removing the 'heads' (tops/seals) of the dirhams.
segments.s216-g95: هما/همه modifies شاه ایران to indicate totality ('the king of all Iran' / 'the whole kingship of Iran'); به رستم marks the recipient ('to Rostam'); سپرد is a past third‑person verb 'entrusted/handed over.' The direct object is omitted here and is recoverable from context (i.e. the charge/treasures).
segments.s216-g96: چنین گفت is a framing formula 'thus said' (چنین = 'thus/so'); what follows is a vocative address. کای is the vocative particle 'O'; نامبردار is an epithet 'the renown‑bearing / renowned one.' The final word گرد is here part of the address (likely a name or archaic epithet) rather than the literal meanings listed in the lemma senses.
segments.s216-g121: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. گرگین is the proper name Gorgin (an epithet/hero). میلاد is the noun 'birth' and here forms a nominal phrase with the name (i.e. 'the birth of Gorgin' / 'when Gorgin was born'). کار آزمای is a compound epithet meaning 'tester of deeds' (کار 'deed' + آزمای, a combining form from آزما(کردن) 'to test'), modifying the named person.
segments.s216-g122: سپه را is the direct object 'the army' marked by the object marker را. زند is the poetic/present-past form of زدن here meaning 'sets/moves/puts in motion' (an archaic literary usage). بر + (بد و نیک) رای means 'upon / according to bad and good counsel' — رای 'counsel/plan' qualified by بد و نیک ('bad and good').
segments.s216-g123: چو is 'when' introducing a temporal clause. خورشید تابنده is a noun phrase 'the shining sun' (noun + participial adjective). بنمود is the literary past of نمودن 'to show/appear' and functions as the clause's verb. چهر 'face' is the direct object (poetic word order places the object after the verb).
segments.s216-g124: بسان بتی is a simile 'like an idol' (بسان = 'like/as', بتی = 'idol' / beautiful face). با دلی پر ز مهر is a prepositional phrase 'with a heart full of love': پر 'full (of)' combines with ز + مهر to mean 'full of love'; دلی is 'a heart' (used adjectivally here).
segments.s216-g125: بر آمد is the verbal compound 'arose / came up' (بر as a verbal prefix + آمد 'came'). خروشیدن is an infinitive/masdar 'the roaring' used nominally, and کرنای specifies the source of the sound 'of the trumpet' (کرنای = trumpet/horn). The whole phrase means 'the trumpet's roar arose.'
segments.s216-g126: تهمتن is an epithet/name ('Tahmētan' / 'the mighty one') functioning as the subject. بیاورد is the past third-person form of آوردن 'he brought/caused to come' used here transitively. لشکر is the direct object 'the army/host' and ز جای is the prepositional phrase 'from their place' (ز = 'from', جای = 'place').
segments.s216-g127: پر اندیشه is an adjectival phrase 'full of thought' (پر = 'full (of)', اندیشه = 'thought'). جان جهاندار شاه is a sequence of epithets: جان 'life/soul', جهاندار 'world-holder / sovereign', شاه 'king' — together forming an honorific apposition 'the king, life of the world (world-holding sovereign)'. The adjective phrase modifies that composite title.
segments.s216-g128: دو فرسنگ is a measure 'two farsangs (parasangs)' indicating distance. با او بیامد is 'came with him' (با 'with' + او 'him' + بیامد past 'came/accompanied'). به‌راه is an adverbial 'on the road / along the way', so the clause says the king accompanied him for two parasangs along the road.
segments.s216-g97: This is a nominal cluster followed by an archaic copula: 'جهان گنج و گنجور' ('the world's treasure and treasury') names what is being spoken of, and 'شمشیر تست' places the predicate at the end: 'the sword is yours.' Here تست is an old poetic copula/possessive meaning roughly 'is yours' (not one of the supplied senses), so the nouns before it are being equated with the sword that belongs to the addressee.
segments.s216-g98: Grammatically this is a genitive phrase plus predicate: 'سر سروران جهان' = 'the heads (i.e. chiefs) of the world's lords' (poetic word order puts سر first), followed by 'زیر تست' meaning 'are beneath you' or 'lie under you.' 'زیر' is used spatially/figuratively ('beneath/under'), and again 'تست' functions as the archaic copula/possessive 'are/are yours' (sense not listed).
segments.s216-g99: 'تو' is the vocative/second‑person pronoun ('you' in a poetic register). 'با' introduces accompaniment ('with'), and 'گرزداران زاولستان' is a noun phrase 'the mace‑bearers of Zavolistan' where the place name follows the group to indicate origin or affiliation (typical Persian post‑modifier order).
segments.s216-g100: A coordinated noun phrase: 'دلیران و شیران کابلستان' — 'the brave ones and the lions of Kabulistan.' The place name 'کابلستان' postmodifies both nouns (common in Persian poetry), and 'شیران' is used metaphorically for valorous fighters ('lions' = brave men) rather than literal animals.
segments.s216-g101: 'همی‌رو' is an archaic present/imperfective form meaning 'go on' or 'keep going.' It is followed by the simile 'به کردار باد دمان' — literally 'in the manner of the wind's hem' — a poetic image meaning 'swiftly like the wind,' where 'کردار' (= manner/conduct) governs the simile and 'دمان' ('hem/skirt') is the poetic locus of the wind.
segments.s216-g102: 'مجوی' is a negative imperative ('do not seek' / 'do not look for'), followed by 'و' linking to 'مفرمای جستن زمان.' 'مفرمای' is a polite/imperative verb ('order/say/do please') and takes the infinitive phrase 'جستن زمان' ('to seek/look for the time' — i.e. to find the right moment) as its object: 'do not search (yourself), but order them to find the time.'
segments.s216-g103: 'ز' is the poetic preposition 'from.' 'گردان شمشیر‌زن' is a compound noun phrase 'a unit/troop of swordsmen' (گردان modified by شمشیر‌زن), and 'سی‌هزار' (thirty thousand) quantifies that group; poetic word order places the numeral after the group it counts.
segments.s216-g104: Another source/direction phrase: 'ز لشکر گزین' = 'from the chosen army/host' where 'گزین' functions adjectivally ('chosen/select') modifying 'لشکر.' 'از در کارزار' is a poetic directional phrase roughly 'into/onto the field of battle' (here از + در indicates movement toward/into the battlefield).
segments.s216-g25: The clause begins with the pronoun تو (you/thou) as the subject. کندی is an archaic/poetic verbal form (here functioning as a past action 'you caused/won/turned'); دل و مغز is a coordinated object ('heart and brain') and دیو سپید is the noun phrase 'the White Demon' modified by سپید. Word order is subject — verb — (coordinated) object phrase with the modifier (سپید) following دیو.
segments.s216-g26: زمانه is personified here as the subject ('fortune/the times'). به مهر تو is a prepositional phrase meaning 'by/through your favour/kindness' (مهر = favour/kindness) that gives the reason or ground, and دارد امید is the present-tense predicate 'has hope/places hope' with دارد as the 3rd‑person singular verb placed before the noun it governs (امید).
segments.s216-g27: زمین گرد is a descriptive compound 'earth‑turning' or 'world‑wide' modifying رخش (the famous steed Rakhsh). ترا is the clitic form (ت + را) attached to رخش and here marks Rakhsh as belonging to/for you ('your Rakhsh' / 'Rakhsh is for you'), and چاکر‌ست is a fused predicate 'is a servant' (چاکر + است) with the copula at the end. Note the modifier precedes the noun and the possessive/clitic appears immediately after the noun before the verb.
segments.s216-g28: زمان‌بر here acts as the subject phrase (literally 'time‑toward' or 'time‑regarding'), تو is the addressed person (dative/oblique clitic), and چون مهربان مادر‌ست is a simile: چون = 'like/as', مهربان مادر = 'a kind mother', and ست is the copula 'is' placed at the end. The whole clause says 'Time toward you is like a kind mother.'
segments.s216-g29: ز marks cause or source ('by/through/because of'). تیغ تو is a genitive/possessive phrase 'your sword' (noun + clitic), خورشید is the subject being affected, and بریان شود is the predicate 'is/gets scorched' with شود (3rd‑person singular) as the verb at the end; the verb form is the classical/presentive 'becomes/is made'.
segments.s216-g30: Parallel structure to the previous clause: ز introduces cause ('by your mace'), گرز تو = 'your mace', ناهید (Anahita/the planet Venus) is the affected subject, and گریان شود is the predicate 'becomes weeping/is made to weep' with شود again as the final verb.
segments.s216-g31: ز marks the causal/source relation ('by/from'). نیروی پیکان is a genitive phrase 'the force/strength of the arrow' (نیروی = 'force/power' governing پیکان). کلک تو follows and here functions as a dependent noun phrase ('your device/trick' or 'your shaft/feather' depending on poetic sense) where تو is the possessive clitic. شیر is the affected noun 'lion'. The whole short phrase groups the instrument/agent that produces the effect on the lion.
segments.s216-g32: به‌روز بلا is a temporal/occasional phrase 'on the day of calamity' (به‌روز = 'on the day', بلا = 'calamity'). گردد is the 3rd‑person verb 'becomes' (classical present/future form) placed after the temporal phrase, and از جنگ سیر is the result/complement 'sated from battle' where از indicates source ('from') and سیر = 'sated/satisfied.' Word order places the temporal phrase before the verb and the resulting state after it.

sentences.s216-s65: Rostam traveled two stages; on one of them he did not rest day or night for a short while.
sentences.s216-s57: He told Fariburz, "Rise at dawn; bring the army forward before the host."
sentences.s216-s58: You must not sleep day and night, unless you become spahbed at Tous's side.
sentences.s216-s59: Tell him: in battle do not be hasty; do not be deceived by the lure of time, and do not be slow.
sentences.s216-s60: Now I will, like the wind, come to the skirts; I will not seek to be a lamb before time.
sentences.s216-s29: Ask me for whatever additional things you need — horses and arms, and also treasure and troops.
sentences.s216-s30: Go with a joyful heart and sound counsel; it is not fitting that such a weak deed be undertaken.
sentences.s216-s31: Rostam replied to the king thus: 'May there never be a gem or crown without you.'
sentences.s216-s32: that with royal glory and exaltation, and with counsel and justice, the heavens do not recall/possess a king like you.
sentences.s216-s53: Fereyberz, give Kay Kāvus ten armies, so that he may be the vanguard and avenger.
sentences.s216-s54: Tahmatan kissed the ground and said that with me the reins and the rider are a matched pair.
sentences.s216-s55: Let us quickly bring the leaders' heads (to us); lest we bring them peace and sleep.
sentences.s216-s56: He began to distribute dirhams to the army, came onto the plain, and made ready for battle.
sentences.s216-s41: May the king be free of sorrow over those slain; may the faces of his ill‑omened men be pale.
sentences.s216-s42: Because of my own Gudarzis my heart is weary; I have girded my waist in the midst of mourning.
sentences.s216-s43: When Kay Khosrow heard his voice, he raised his face, and from his two eyes two streams (of tears) flowed.
sentences.s216-s44: He said to him, 'Without you I want nothing — neither throne nor crown, nor mace nor bow.'
sentences.s216-s37: Suffering both from thirst and from the long road, to taste hardship instead of pampering.
sentences.s216-s38: I have seen so much such pain and hardship that I have never once inquired after joy.
sentences.s216-s39: You are a king of new custom, and I, like a wayfarer, am shut in between whenever you give the command.
sentences.s216-s40: May I, with an army, gird my loins and turn this misfortune away from the Iranians.
sentences.s216-s21: Whoever among them has escaped with his life — all are worn out at the mountain Homaun.
sentences.s216-s22: All have bowed their heads toward the sky, toward the Creator, Lord of place and time.
sentences.s216-s23: that the so‑called Piltan should come here by the power of God and by my command.
sentences.s216-s24: On that dark night when I summoned this messenger, I poured out much blood from my heart.
sentences.s216-s1: After that, when news came to the king that the elders had become victorious in battle.
sentences.s216-s2: The spahbed withdrew to Mount Homāvan; many of the host scattered and vanished.
sentences.s216-s3: In Goudarz's palace the household/followers were left empty of troops and free men.
sentences.s216-s4: The star lamented over them, and at their bedside blood welled up.
sentences.s216-s25: For three days I did not tell these words to anyone, except before the Creator who answers cries.
sentences.s216-s26: Now the affair has gone beyond measure; my heart, because of these words, became full of care.
sentences.s216-s27: The army's and the spahbed's hope rests on you — may you be bright‑souled and sound in body.
sentences.s216-s28: May your head be green and your heart joyful; may Zāl's body be far from evil and from suspicion.
sentences.s216-s33: Khosrow has heard that Kay Qobad put a large hat on his head.
sentences.s216-s34: For Iran, in vengeance I have girded my loins; I have not sat a single day in peace.
sentences.s216-s35: Whether the wilderness or the darkness, the demon or the lion, whether magic or the brave dragon.
sentences.s216-s36: That very battle of Turan and Mazandaran — the dark night and heavy maces.
sentences.s216-s17: So long as you set a hat upon a man, the enemy did not so much as glance toward Iran.
sentences.s216-s18: Now Giv and Goudarz and Tous and many chiefs from this frontier (are) the diggers.
sentences.s216-s19: All — hearts full of blood and eyes full of water (tears) — are fleeing from Afrasiab's Turks.
sentences.s216-s20: Many of Goudarz's men were slain; their bodies became the earth's bedding on the battlefield plain.
sentences.s216-s5: Because of them the world is full of dust and blood, and the lofty star of Tūs has become fallen.
sentences.s216-s6: He commanded that Rostam go to Piltan; Rostam strode into the court with the assembly.
sentences.s216-s7: All the wise men of Iran departed — the worldly‑wise and renowned mobeds.
sentences.s216-s8: The chief of the illustrious opened his tongue (began to speak) and recalled many of the army's battles.
sentences.s216-s9: He said to Rostam thus: “O exalted one, I fear that this fortune will not last long.”
sentences.s216-s10: My heart continually turns toward decline; because of his deeds my heart became full of alarm.
sentences.s216-s11: You, fosterer of crown and throne — the radiance of fortune draws from you the luck of the world‑ruler.
sentences.s216-s12: The heart of the wheel (fate) is on the tip of your sword; heaven, time, and earth are beneath you.
sentences.s216-s45: May the sky lie beneath the bend of your lasso; may the heads of crowned rulers be in your fetters.
sentences.s216-s46: From dinars and treasure, and from crown and jewel — helmet and bow and lasso and girdle.

sentences.s216-s48: The king of all Iran entrusted everything to Rostam; thus he said, 'O renown‑bearer, ...'
sentences.s216-s61: When Gorgin, the tester of deeds, was born, he set the host in motion according to both ill and good counsel.
sentences.s216-s62: When the shining sun showed its face, like an idol, with a heart full of love.
sentences.s216-s63: There arose the roar of the trumpet; Tahmten caused the host to move from their place.
sentences.s216-s64: The world-holding king, full of thought and soul, accompanied him for two parasangs on the road.
sentences.s216-s49: The world's treasure and its treasury — the sword is yours; the heads of the world's rulers lie beneath you.
sentences.s216-s50: You, with the mace‑bearers of Zavolistan, and the brave men and lion‑like warriors of Kabulistan.
sentences.s216-s51: Press on like the wind along the hem; do not linger, but command (them) to seek the right moment.
sentences.s216-s52: From the battalions of swordsmen, thirty thousand; from the chosen host, into the field of battle.
sentences.s216-s13: You have won the heart and brain of the White Demon; Fortune places its hope in your favour.
sentences.s216-s14: The earth‑turning Rakhsh is your servant; Time toward you is like a kind mother.
sentences.s216-s15: By your sword the sun is scorched; by your mace Anahita is made to weep.
sentences.s216-s16: By the force of your arrow‑device the lion, on the day of calamity, becomes sated from battle.
